question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, further to his recent announcement of £8 million funding for dental services, to detail on the new Emergency Dental Clinic to be established in the Western Health and Social Care Trust to provide urgent care for patients who cannot access routine services.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of pitches allocated to (a) GAA; (b) soccer; (c) rugby; (d) hockey; and (e) any additional sports; and (ii) any criteria used to determine allocation, at the Strule Shared Education Campus.

To ask the Minister of Education whether any assessment has been made of the process used to determine the location and number of pitches for GAA, soccer, rugby, hockey, and any additional sports at the Strule Shared Education Campus.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the process for appeal when care is removed or reduced by the Early Review Team.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any services and provisions available for adults with suspected ADHD; and (ii) how individuals can access these services and provisions as they proceed towards formal diagnosis.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the remit for the Early Review Team (ERT) in the Western Health and Social Care Trust; (ii) the number of people who have had care removed or reduced since the formation of the ERT; and (iii) any assessment that the ERT must conduct when reducing or removing care.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department's process for handling prescriptions initially issued by a GP in the South of Ireland but now required in the North, specifically in relation to melatonin prescribed for children.
answer_text: Prescriptions written by a clinician in the Republic of Ireland cannot automatically be continued within GP services in Northern Ireland.The clinician who signs a prescription is legally responsible for it and can only prescribe within their own skills and professional role. Because of this, if a patient moves or presents with a prescription from the Republic of Ireland, the Northern Ireland GP must make their own new decision about whether to prescribe.Before issuing a prescription, the GP must be satisfied that:the medication is clinically appropriate,there is enough reliable clinical information to support it, andsafe arrangements are in place for monitoring the treatment going forward.Melatonin is classified within Northern Ireland as an ‘amber list medication’ for paediatric use. Under this classification, initiation of treatment falls under the responsibility of secondary care, with any ongoing prescribing in primary care undertaken only within the context of a formal shared care agreement.  In line with current guidance, GPs refer children to paediatric services for specialist assessment to determine whether melatonin treatment is appropriate.The Regional Group for Specialist Medicines (RGSM) produces professional guidance for clinicians on prescribing responsibilities for a defined list of specialist medicines, known as the Red/Amber List.Amber List medicines are those medicines where the responsibility for prescribing may be safely transferred within the HSC from secondary to primary care, provided that a shared care agreement is established in advance with the patient’s GP.This process ensures that any treatment is initiated and managed in accordance with established regional safety and governance frameworks. As clinical and legal liability associated with a prescribed medication falls to the doctor who issues and signs the script, a GP must be confident in the diagnosis, support and guidance given to them and the patient, by the recommending specialist.  The decision to enter into a shared care agreement, or not, is therefore a clinical decision for individual GPs to make.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any financial support that will be made available for the most vulnerable in our society following recent increases in energy prices.
answer_text: I refer you to the answer to AQW 41656/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount of funding his Department plans to allocate to Fleadh Cheoil na hÉireann 2026, and events surrounding it.
answer_text: My Department is supporting the Fleadh Cheoil na hÉireann in various ways through direct engagement with partners and also several of the Departmental arm’s-length bodies.  There is no dedicated funding allocation planned.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans he has for investing in local traditional bands and musicians ahead of Fleadh Cheoil na hÉireann 2026, to assist Comhaltas competitors in having the best chance at success.
answer_text: My Department is investing in the Fleadh through the support provided by several of the arm-length bodies of my Department.  A separate investment arrangement for traditional bands and musicians is not planned beyond current provisions already in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any Fleadh Cheoil na hÉireann 2026 events he will be attending in his official capacity as Minister.
answer_text: I will consider any invitations received on a case-by-case basis and as diary permits.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans his Department is putting in place to support and promote Fleadh Cheoil na hÉireann 2026.
answer_text: Several arms-length bodies of my Department and much of the cultural sector are already engaged with partners to support and promote Fleadh Cheoil na hÉireann 2026.  The particular focus is on developing and promoting the fringe programme of events which will showcase the diverse cultural sector we have in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether he has any plans to expand the Hospital at Home service to Omagh; and (ii) to detail why this service had not been included in the initial roll out.
answer_text: Based on the current funding allocation, the Western Health and Social Care Trust have profiled their operational arrangements to deliver a Hospital at Home Service in Fermanagh, Derry/Londonderry, Limavady and Strabane localities. Should this position change, the Western Trust will consider future service delivery areas.A review of Intermediate Care Services led by the Chief Nursing Officer was undertaken across 2023 and early 2024 and included the core function of Hospital at Home provision. A project report has been developed, and has been agreed with a range of recommendations to be taken forward in partnership with my department and Executive Service Leads across all areas in geographical Trusts in the next phase of this work.An Intermediate Care Steering Group was established in September 2025 to take forward transformation of Intermediate Care Services and a new Intermediate Care Hospital at Home Regional Working Group agreed. The main purpose of the working group is to standardise the provision of Hospital at Home services across the region, ensuring equity of access, maximising rehabilitation opportunities and reducing or delaying reliance on longer term services for all.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action being taken to ensure that people who live in rural areas are not left without the palliative care and support they need at the end of life.
answer_text: Being able to access palliative and end of life care and support when needed is important for everyone, no matter where they live or whether their care is being provided at home or in any other setting. The regional Palliative Care in Partnership Programme, co-led by my Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group and the Public Health Agency and comprising a wide range of stakeholders, provides regional direction for the development and improvement of palliative and end of life care in Northern Ireland. At both a regional level, and through its Palliative Care Locality Boards, the Partnership Programme has been central to developing person-centred palliative care services.The development of the Palliative Care Key Worker role has been crucial in supporting those who have been identified as likely to be in their last year of life.  Irrespective of where people live, people with palliative care needs who have been identified as likely to be in their last year of life will have a Palliative Care Keyworker (typically the District Nurse) who can be contacted for additional support when required.  The role of the Keyworker includes the planning and co-ordination of care - anticipating patient needs and co-ordination planning often mitigates against escalations, helping people to remain at home in the community.Palliative care is supported through seven day District Nursing available in all Trusts - and available 24/7 in two Trust localities.  In addition, all HSC Trusts have contracts in place with Marie Curie to provide out of hours Rapid Response Services (RRS), which include weekend and bank holiday cover. The RRS aims to provide a prompt and timely response to calls for care and assistance, telephone advice to families and patients who may not need a home visit, and urgent at home care to patients. Whilst recognising the improvements in palliative care that have been made, further progress is essential to ensure palliative care services meet the needs of our population across Northern Ireland.My Department has commenced a baseline scoping exercise to consider adult palliative care services.  The project will seek to fully understand the performance of existing services across the region, and whether they are meeting the palliative and end of life care needs of the populations they currently serve, and those they will need to be prepared to serve in the future.The exercise will also include an updated population based palliative and end of life care needs assessment.  This will have a particular focus on vulnerable groups and people facing inequalities, including looking at rural and urban populations. Importantly, the scoping exercise will also seek to understand the experience and quality of palliative and end of life care being delivered across care settings and in all areas.  The scoping exercise will help specify existing services and identify gaps, unmet needs, and opportunities for improvement which will inform future planning and c

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how people who live in rural areas of West Tyrone will be supported to remain in their home while still accessing essential end of life care.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail how his Department's plans to address the lack of carers in rural settings, especially in West Tyrone; and (ii) for his assessment of the situation facing people who require care in the home but cannot access the proper support and services.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the names and contact details of care provider organisations who assist the Western Health and Social Care Trust in providing care to people in their own homes; and (ii) the number of people currently receiving home care; (ii) the levels of care provided; and (iv) the number of people currently waiting for home care to become available, in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Below is a list of the care provider organisations who assist the WHSCT in providing care to people in their own homesHomecare ProviderContact DetailsBryson Care1st Floor, Block 1 Glendermott Valley Business ParkChurch Brae Londonderry BT47 3QJPhone: 02871 313512CareplusUnit 62-63 Enniskillen Business Centre, 21 Lackaghboy Rd, EnniskillenBT74 4RLPhone: 02866 325688Connected HealthUnit 2 Aghanloo Industrial Estate Limavady BT49 0HEPhone 028777 67604Glen CaringOrchard Road Industrial Estate Strabane BT82 9FRPhone: 02871 885155Lakeland Community Care1 Lattone Rd Belcoo Enniskillen BT93 5AQPhone: 02866 386934North West Care67 Main St Ballykelly Limavady BT49 9HSPhone: 028777 23514Strabane & District Caring Services34 - 36 Bridge St Strabane BT82 9AEPhone: 02871 884986(ii) The total number of individuals in receipt of care as at 28th October 2025 is 3863.(iii)  The minimal homecare package delivered by the Western Trust is 30 mins per week, (for a weekly visit to support with a shower), the maximum is 98 hours per week.  The average number of hours is 10.7 per week this represents 26 weekly care visits.(iv)  As at 28th October 2025 there were 353 individuals waiting for a full package of care and 324 waiting for a partial package (i.e. increase to established care arrangements already in place).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the current waiting time for an autism assessment in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health whether a retrospective application can be made to the Fair Play Small Grant Scheme in exceptional circumstances.
answer_text: The Department of Health, through the Childcare Partnership, facilitates an annual grant scheme to ensure children with additional needs are included in early years services, known as Fair Play. The application process for 2025/26 opened on 4th June and closed on 30th June and no retrospective applications can be made as all funds available for 25/26 have been allocated for this financial year. Any funds returned in year are allocated to the next application on the existing waiting list in rank scoring order. The funding provides a contribution towards additional staffing capacity. The focus is on the individual needs of children who are accessing quality play and learning experiences in registered community, voluntary and private provision throughout the academic year.The value of the Fair Play fund April 2025 - March 2026 is £824,000 which includes funding from the DoH, DE and the Childcare Partnership via the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG). DE funding has been made available through the forthcoming Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy. This resource does not meet all the requests in full, therefore, the application process is competitive. This is an unintended consequence of the fund due to its size and the level of support needed in the early years sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of successful applications to the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme; and (ii) the types of surgery applied for and agreed, for those who live in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the two remaining sections of the A5 road which have not yet been decided upon.
answer_text: Work continues in relation to addressing the Planning Appeals Commission recommendations on the remaining two sections of the scheme, i.e. Section 1 (south of Strabane to New Buildings) and Section 3 Phase 3 (Ballygawley to the border). These will be subject to separate decisions in due course.  A cross-border working group has now been established to reinforce collaboration on the continued development of the N2 Clontibret to border Road Scheme and Phase 3 of the A5, from Ballygawley to the border. Both Administrations continue to closely collaborate on the A5 / Donegal TEN-T projects interface, including in relation to the mitigation of impacts on the River Foyle floodplain, with regular meetings taking place with Donegal County Council and the Office of Public Works.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the appeal of the High Court ruling on the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme.
answer_text: The A5WTC High Court judgment received on 23 June 2025 was hugely disappointing, not just for me, but for all the families and campaigners such as Enough is Enough who have been extremely diligent and steadfast in their commitment to ensuring that the road is built. My thoughts are always with all those families and the unimaginable grief of losing loved ones and those who are living with injuries as a result of collisions along this road. My Department has lodged an appeal against the recent judgment, this appeal will be heard in December 2025. Not only is the appeal considered necessary to get the road built and save lives, it will also seek to resolve important points of law that will affect all parties concerned. While my immediate priority remains the preparation of a robust appeal, the ultimate aim is to ensure the delivery of this flagship project, and I am determined to do all that I can to ensure that happens so that no more lives are lost on the A5.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on any work being carried out by his Department to amend marriage law.
answer_text: A draft Marriage and Civil Partnership Bill is in the latter stages of preparation. This important Bill will raise the minimum age of marriage and civil partnership to 18 and will bring belief marriages into the statutory framework. I anticipate that a Bill will be introduced in the Assembly by the end of this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the criteria that currently exists to allow for admission to Sure Start facilities for children who have Special Education Needs but live outside the catchment area.
answer_text: 2025-26 Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) funding will enable children and their families living outside current catchment areas, to avail of Sure Start support through introduction of a targeted outreach model. Criteria for the outreach model are currently being developed by the Department of Health which administers the Sure Start programme on behalf of the Department of Education. The outreach model aims to ensure that children with demonstrable need can be identified and referred into the programme by Health and Social Care Professionals, regardless of where they live in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide an update on the development of the A32 Cornamuck Road, Omagh to Enniskillen.
answer_text: I was delighted to be able to announce the progression of the A32 Cornamuck Road Scheme last week. This upgrade on such an essential route, linking the main towns of Tyrone and Fermanagh, seeks to reduce journey times, increase reliability, and improve road safety, while also providing greater connectivity to both acute hospital services and educational facilities for local communities.My Department also has two road maintenance schemes planned for the A32 route. These include a 480m carriageway resurfacing scheme at the Tummery Road between Dromore and Irvinestown and a 1.9km surface dressing scheme at Clanabogan Road between Omagh and Dromore being delivered this summer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) what actions he and his Department have taken to reduce waiting lists for a diagnosis of (a) ADHD; and (ii) autism spectrum disorder; and (ii) what arrangements can be put in place to allow patients to access medication as part of Shared Care Arrangements.
answer_text: Waiting lists for a diagnosis of Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) and autism spectrum disorder (ASD) are lengthy and growing for children, young people and adults across Northern Ireland. This is unacceptable and my Department is undertaking a number of actions in an effort to improve the situation within a financially constrained environment.(i) There is currently no specifically commissioned service for ADHD in Northern Ireland. While there is some provision available across the Health and Social Care system which has evolved in response to population demand and expertise within Health & Social Care teams, it is inconsistent and there are lengthy waiting lists across Trusts. In response, my Department has commissioned a needs assessment on ADHD, the findings of which will help inform options for the development of a commissioned ADHD service across Trusts, subject to future budget availability.  This needs assessment, which covers both adults and children, aims to determine the level of demand for a commissioned service, as well as providing recommendations around what such a service might look like, including potential implementation and staffing costs. This work is anticipated to be completed by September 2025 and will help inform future development of ADHD service provision that is resourced to meet increased demand for assessment.(ii) In respect to ASD services, we are committed to working closely and collaboratively with Health and Social Care Trusts to improve access and develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidence based services that meet NICE guidelines for ASD (CG128 & CG142).  Within Children services, we have recently launched a public consultation on a draft Children’s and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework which can be accessed on the Department of Health website. It is envisaged that long waiting times for ASD diagnosis in childhood will be addressed through the implementation of this framework.In conjunction with the Public Health Agency and Child Health colleagues, my Department is developing a regionally agreed needs- based neurodevelopmental pathway. This will allow individual needs to be assessed within a regionally consistent framework and will promote opportunities for earlier intervention to be provided without the need for a diagnosis. This framework will support development of a regionally consistent and defined neurodevelopmental pathway for children and young people that complies with NICE guidelines.Within Adult services, my Department has recruited a project lead for Adult Autism who took up post in March 2025. This role will provide regional leadership and a dedicated focus on enhancing and improving services for autistic adults and their families across Northern Ireland.We recently completed an initial scoping and engagement exercise with HSC Trusts to map existing Adult Autism diagnostic and support pathways and processes and identify what is wo

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the delivery of an Irish Language Strategy.
answer_text: Delivery of an Irish Language Strategy, and an Ulster-Scots Language, Heritage and Culture Strategy, shall follow completion of the development phase for both strategies. 		The timetable for this delivery is largely outside my control given the range of material to be reviewed by each Executive Department within the work programme of the Cross-Departmental Working Group.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline how a patient who meets the criteria for the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme begins the process of (i) making an application; and (ii) accessing funding.

To ask the Minister of Health to outline the necessary criteria that patients must meet to qualify for the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme; and (ii) where and when this information will be published.
To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on any discussions he has had with the Secretary of State for Work and Pensions regarding the proposed changes to benefits.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any impact from the Communities in Transition project in building confident and resilient communities.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether he is aware of concerns from local glass manufacturers in relation to the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs' proposed Extender Producer Responsibility fees; and (ii) whether he is exploring solutions to address these.
answer_text: I am aware of concerns from local glass manufacturers in relation to proposed Extended Producer Responsibility for packaging (pEPR) illustrative base fees. I am exploring solutions to address these concerns and have actively held meetings with local glass manufacturers to discuss their challenges. I will also continue to review the approach to illustrative base fees in response to industry feedback. I have communicated concerns around fees for glass manufacturers to DEFRA with discussions on this matter ongoing.	My officials will also continue to engage with industry and across the other nations as work on pEPR illustrative base fees progresses.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline what measures his Department is taking to support voluntary playgroup settings.
answer_text: I have approved significant increases in the funding rate for non-statutory pre-school settings participating in the Pre-school Education Programme.  This has resulted in the funding rate increasing from £2,150 to £2,550 per pupil per year from September 2024, which is the largest single increase in the funding rate my Department has ever made.In August 2024, I also approved the payment of a one-off non-consolidated payment of £100 per pupil for non-statutory pre-school settings. This additional funding means non-statutory pre-school settings will receive approximately £3.2m more in the 2024-25 academic year.  For an average non-statutory setting providing 16 funded pre-school places, this represents additional funding of £8,000 per annum.The Department for the Economy is leading on a scoping exercise to develop a clearer picture of the business support requirements across the broader early learning and childcare sector, including voluntary and community providers.This work is due to conclude in March 2025 and will inform proposals within the draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy, which I intend to bring forward to the Executive in autumn 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the recruitment process for the appointment of the role of Education Advisor at the Strule Shared Education Campus; (ii) the key stages of the process, including significant dates; and (iii) the job specification and any other essential criteria associated with this post.
answer_text: The role of Education Advisor to the Strule Shared Education Campus Programme is a secondment opportunity rather than a recruitment. The secondment to the programme is for three years after which the secondee will return to their substantive employment. The opportunity was advertised through the Northern Ireland Civil Service Interchange Scheme.The key stages of the secondment opportunity process were as follows:Advertised by the Interchange Scheme from 12th September to 18th October 2024.The selection process was paused when it became apparent that the post had not been advertised sufficiently widely enough in and had not therefore reached the intended target audience.Re-advertised from 1st November to 22nd November 2024 and applications were sifted on 18th December 2024.Informal discussions took place in Omagh on 5th February 2025 and the successful candidate was informed by telephone on 6th February.(iii)	The job description for the post and the interchange hosting proforma have been deposited in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.
answer_text: I intend to bring forward a comprehensive and ambitious Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) Strategy for Executive consideration and public consultation by autumn 2025.  This work is being supported by the cross departmental ELC Task and Finish Group, and a wide range of stakeholders.The priority in the interim will be to continue and, where budget permits, expand the measures put in place this year.I secured £25m Executive funding in May 2024 to implement a package of measures for 2024/25.  This investment is making a real difference to thousands of children and families right across Northern Ireland.  	I have implemented a Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) that has provided working parents with a 15% reduction on their childcare bills.  To date, over 14,000 children have registered for the scheme and parents have saved almost £5.5m.  When combined with Tax-Free Childcare (TFC) the total saving for parents is over £11.3m.	I have invested an additional £7.6m in existing early years programmes like Sure Start and I have initiated a major expansion of pre-school education so that an additional 2,500 children will get a full-time place in September 2025 with more to follow in successive years.  This exceeds the target we had set ourselves back in May 2024.	I commissioned NISRA to undertake a major household survey of parents’ childcare requirements.  It is now complete and the results are expected in April/May 2025. This and the administrative data being collected from the NICSS will inform the development of the full strategy.  So too will the work being carried out by the Department for the Economy, to assess the sustainability challenges faced by childcare providers and determine what support they need and how government should respond. 	I have been able to secure £50m for early learning and childcare within the draft Executive Budget for 2025/26.  This will allow me to continue the NICSS next year.  I am currently considering what else is affordable within the allocation.  The funding and full package of measures for 2025/26 will be finalised when we agree it as an Executive.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the consultation following the report on Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses.
To ask the Minister of Education to outline what his Department and the Education Authority are doing to protect school transport services when drivers are absent due to sickness.
answer_text: I understand the difficulties it causes for children and families when a school bus service is disrupted, especially when this is at short notice.The delivery of home to school transport on a day-to-day basis is the responsibility of the Education Authority. It has a range of contingency plans in place to keep services operating.  However, in the case of illness of a driver or bus escort, which may not be reported until the morning of the run, it can be very challenging to secure appropriate cover on time to avoid disruption. While staff absence due to illness is the main factor, service disruption can also be caused by challenging winter weather conditions, mechanical issues and in recent academic years due to periods of industrial action. Contingency measures include calling upon emergency relief drivers and escorts and the use private hire contracts and agency staff.  In addition, the EA has an ongoing recruitment programme for drivers and escorts across its transport service.There is, however, an industry wide shortage of drivers in Northern Ireland and this means that despite all efforts, it is not always possible for the EA to make alternative arrangements to cover unexpected periods of absence.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to previous correspondence and recent flooding, to detail his Department's assessment of possible measures that will stop river water from filling The Dry Arch and flooding its basement at Campsie Bridge, Omagh.
answer_text: My Department instructed its Partnering consultant to undertake a comprehensive feasibility study for the ‘Dry Arch’ which assessed various technical solutions and options. The feasibility study for the ‘Dry Arch’ concluded that whilst there was no viable engineering option to alleviate flooding, it did recommend a ‘Reactive Flood Plan’ option where a pump would be deployed in the ‘Dry Arch’ to effectively draw down flood levels during flooding and reduce the impact on properties.  My Department has subsequently constructed a drainage channel and sumps within the arch. This has effectively facilitated the pumping operation process here, which was utilised during  Storm Bert and  helped mitigate against more serious flooding. To enhance my Department’s response as part of the ‘Reactive Flood Plan’ two new higher velocity mobile pumps for the ‘Dry Arch’ have been purchased and scheduled for delivery before the end of December 2024. In addition, and following the most recent flooding event, my officials have issued a further brief to their Partner consultant to review the information within the original feasibility study, to investigate the suitability of the possible solution put forward by business owners and identify any other potential solutions to prevent water ingress into the enclosed area within the bridge.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has any plans to alleviate flooding issues at Campsie Bridge, Omagh.
answer_text: My Department instructed its Partnering consultant to undertake a comprehensive feasibility study for the ‘Dry Arch’ which assessed various technical solutions and options. The feasibility study for the ‘Dry Arch’ concluded that whilst there was no viable engineering option to alleviate flooding, it did recommend a ‘Reactive Flood Plan’ option where a pump would be deployed in the ‘Dry Arch’ to effectively draw down flood levels during flooding and reduce the impact on properties.  My Department has subsequently constructed a drainage channel and sumps within the arch. This has effectively facilitated the pumping operation process here, which was utilised during  Storm Bert and  helped mitigate against more serious flooding. To enhance my Department’s response as part of the ‘Reactive Flood Plan’ two new higher velocity mobile pumps for the ‘Dry Arch’ have been purchased and scheduled for delivery before the end of December 2024.
