question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any work undertaken by local departmental officials to assess the level of flood risk to nearby businesses at Campsie Bridge, Omagh.
answer_text: My Department instructed its Partnering consultant to undertake a comprehensive feasibility study for the ‘Dry Arch’ which assessed various technical solutions and options. The feasibility study for the ‘Dry Arch’ concluded that whilst there was no viable engineering option to alleviate flooding, it did recommend a ‘Reactive Flood Plan’ option where a pump would be deployed in the ‘Dry Arch’ to effectively draw down flood levels during flooding and reduce the impact on properties.  My Department has subsequently constructed a drainage channel and sumps within the arch. This has effectively facilitated the pumping operation process here, which was utilised during  Storm Bert and  helped mitigate against more serious flooding. To enhance my Department’s response as part of the ‘Reactive Flood Plan’ two new higher velocity mobile pumps for the ‘Dry Arch’ have been purchased and scheduled for delivery before the end of December 2024. In addition, and following the most recent flooding event, my officials have issued a further brief to their Partner consultant to review the information within the original feasibility study, to investigate the suitability of the possible solution put forward by business owners and identify any other potential solutions to prevent water ingress into the enclosed area within the bridge.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Investment Strategy for Northern Ireland.

To ask the Minister of Health whether (i) Emotional Wellbeing Teams in Schools will be available to schools in the academic year beginning in September 2025; and (ii) all schools will have access to this programme.
answer_text: The Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework was jointly launched by Department of Health and Department of Education in 2021.  The main emphasis of the Framework is to support educational settings to promote emotional health and wellbeing at a universal level. A number of important projects and pilots have been taken forward through this Framework aimed at various elements of providing help and support to children and young people and their emotional and mental health. A key project under the Framework is the Emotional Wellbeing Teams in Schools Framework (EWTS), which launched in academic year 2023/2024 to post Primary Schools.  The aim is to provide intervention at the earliest opportunity by ensuring schools are aware of appropriate pathways for support in their local area and within CAMHS. This work involves working collaboratively with teaching staff and other providers to coordinate and deliver wellbeing programmes and interventions. In the current academic year (2024/25) EWTS will engage with 68 schools across the region. The continuation of the Framework beyond 2024/25 including EWTS is unknown at present as it is dependent on budget availability within both the Department of Education and my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether (i) Emotional Wellbeing Teams in Schools will be available to schools in the academic year beginning in September 2025; and (ii) all schools will have access to this programme.
answer_text: The Emotional Wellbeing Teams in Schools programme is one of several projects / services jointly funded by the Department of Education (DE) and the Department of Health (DoH) under the joint DE / DoH Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework.The Emotional Wellbeing Teams in Schools programme is a health-led initiative and is available to mainstream post-primary schools, providing support to participating schools for one academic year.  The budgetary position for the 2025/26 year is not yet known, continuation of all projects will be dependent on budget availability.  Implementation of the Framework projects is overseen by a cross-departmental / agency Programme Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans to make the Emotional Wellbeing Teams in Schools a permanent support in prevention and early intervention strategies for schools.
answer_text: The Emotional Wellbeing Teams in Schools programme is one of several projects / services jointly funded by the Department of Education (DE) and the Department of Health (DoH) under the joint DE / DoH Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework.The Emotional Wellbeing Teams in Schools programme is a health-led initiative and is available to mainstream post-primary schools, providing support to participating schools for one academic year.  The budgetary position for the 2025/26 year is not yet known, continuation of all projects will be dependent on budget availability.  Implementation of the Framework projects is overseen by a cross-departmental / agency Programme Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to make funding available beyond September 2025 for Emotional Wellbeing Teams in Schools to ensure all schools have access to the program.
answer_text: The Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework was jointly launched by Department of Health and Department of Education in 2021.  The main emphasis of the Framework is to support educational settings to promote emotional health and wellbeing at a universal level. A number of important projects and pilots have been taken forward through this Framework aimed at various elements of providing help and support to children and young people and their emotional and mental health. A key project under the Framework is the Emotional Wellbeing Teams in Schools Framework (EWTS), which launched in academic year 2023/2024 to post Primary Schools.  The aim is to provide intervention at the earliest opportunity by ensuring schools are aware of appropriate pathways for support in their local area and within CAMHS. This work involves working collaboratively with teaching staff and other providers to coordinate and deliver wellbeing programmes and interventions. In the current academic year (2024/25) EWTS will engage with 68 schools across the region. The continuation of the Framework beyond 2024/25 including EWTS is unknown at present as it is dependent on budget availability within both the Department of Education and my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans to make the Emotional Wellbeing Teams in Schools a permanent support in prevention and early intervention strategies for schools.
answer_text: The Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework was jointly launched by Department of Health and Department of Education in 2021.  The main emphasis of the Framework is to support educational settings to promote emotional health and wellbeing at a universal level. A number of important projects and pilots have been taken forward through this Framework aimed at various elements of providing help and support to children and young people and their emotional and mental health. A key project under the Framework is the Emotional Wellbeing Teams in Schools Framework (EWTS), which launched in academic year 2023/2024 to post Primary Schools.  The aim is to provide intervention at the earliest opportunity by ensuring schools are aware of appropriate pathways for support in their local area and within CAMHS. This work involves working collaboratively with teaching staff and other providers to coordinate and deliver wellbeing programmes and interventions. In the current academic year (2024/25) EWTS will engage with 68 schools across the region. The continuation of the Framework beyond 2024/25 including EWTS is unknown at present as it is dependent on budget availability within both the Department of Education and my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to make funding available beyond September 2025 for Emotional Wellbeing Teams in Schools to ensure all schools have access to the program.
answer_text: The Emotional Wellbeing Teams in Schools programme is one of several projects / services jointly funded by the Department of Education (DE) and the Department of Health (DoH) under the joint DE / DoH Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework.The Emotional Wellbeing Teams in Schools programme is a health-led initiative and is available to mainstream post-primary schools, providing support to participating schools for one academic year.  The budgetary position for the 2025/26 year is not yet known, continuation of all projects will be dependent on budget availability.  Implementation of the Framework projects is overseen by a cross-departmental / agency Programme Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the progress of legislation to be brought forward to amend the Matrimonial Causes (Northern Ireland) Order 1978 to allow applications for a divorce without apportioning blame on either party.
answer_text: Work to amend our current system of divorce began earlier this year with a review of the corresponding legislation in other jurisdictions. In the coming months, officials plan to meet with a selection of local stakeholders to take their initial views on divorce law reform. It is my intention that these soundings will, in turn, inform a full public consultation in 2025, the results of which will help shape legislation to make the divorce process less acrimonious.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, given that cross-compliance penalties pursued for pollution incidents by his Department are a civil matter, and that those who have been suspected of polluting cannot be identified, whether incidents successfully pursued under this mechanism are withheld from official pollution incident statistics published by his Department.
answer_text: DAERA and NIEA’s official water pollution incident statistics are published annually by the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency in The Northern Ireland Statistics Report. The 2024 Edition is available at the following link:https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-environmental-statistics-report-2024 The report contains statistics and information on a number of environmental indicators covering key themes and includes information on the number, source and severity of water pollution incidents investigated by NIEA. The Report does not provide statistics on prosecution and other enforcement outcomes for water pollution offences. Enforcement information in respect of High and Medium Severity water pollution incidents can be provided by NIEA on request. Enforcement information provided by NIEA in respect of High and Medium Severity water pollution incidents will include instances where the enforcement outcome has been a cross compliance penalty, however details of individuals will not be provided. As low severity incidents are not progressed for enforcement action, but may be progressed for cross compliance penalty, such incidents are not included in the reported enforcement statistics.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the development of wildfire policy following public consultations on (i) the Reducing Wildfires in the Countryside Plan 2018; and (ii) Wildfires in NI: Draft Strategic Way Forward 2023.
answer_text: The Reducing Wildfires in the Countryside Plan 2018 helped inform the make up and development of the NI Strategic Wildfire Group and the NI Wildfire Forum; these groups were established in late 2022 and early 2023 respectively. The Reducing Wildfires in the Countryside Plan 2018, which included a public consultation and workshop involving national and international wildfire experts, provided feedback which helped inform the development of the content of the draft Strategic Way Forward 2023.The Wildfires in NI: Draft Strategic Way Forward 2023, was consulted on from April 2023 to June 2023 and received 46 responses from across N.I., GB, Europe and further afield. The responses to this consultation have been assessed and incorporated into a revised version of the Wildfires in NI: Draft Strategic Way Forward 2024. This updated version has been agreed by the NI Strategic Wildfire Group. Officials are currently preparing the document for my consideration.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether the promotion of regional balance and supporting rural communities is a core aspect of the Investment Strategy.
answer_text: The strategy identifies the priorities for investment at a high level.  It recognises the need for regional balance and the need to consider the specific requirements of rural communities as part of the core objectives.  It identifies the need to enhance both our built and rural environments and highlights the role that improving transport links and continued investment in digital connectivity can play in helping rural communities to thrive.A Rural Needs Impact Assessment was carried out prior to consultation on the draft Investment Strategy.  This found it to be consistent with and supportive of the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs proposed Rural Policy Framework and its objectives. Ensuring that all areas are supported to achieve economic growth and prosperity is a key priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Chicago and Toronto trade mission.
answer_text: I travelled to North America with twelve of the norths most innovative companies, representing sectors including financial services, insurance, education, green energy, and manufacturing. Over the course of the week, company representatives undertook almost 100 one-to-one meetings with potential clients. Feedback from the trade delegates was universally positive, with many of the companies reporting solid new business opportunities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the Fiscal Council.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether (i) his Department; and (ii) the College of Agriculture Food & Rural Enterprise have any plans to deliver the use of antimicrobials training courses, that had been free for farmers, farm family members and farm workers up until 31 October 2023.
answer_text: My Department through CAFRE facilitated ‘Responsible Use of Antimicrobials’ training for dairy, beef & sheep producers from January 2019 to October 2023, under the Farm Family Key Skills scheme which was part of the Northern Ireland Rural Development programme. During this time over 13,250 farmers, farm family members and farm employees from over 11,500 farm businesses undertook the training. I currently have no plans to recommence delivery of ‘Responsible use of Antimicrobials’ training.  For those that currently require this training, a range of other commercial training providers are available and listed on the LMCNI and Red Tractor websites.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, following his commitment to invest £7.1 million in early years provision, for a timeline on plans to expand core early years programmes such as Sure Start.
answer_text: I am pleased to have secured Executive agreement to provide £25m funding for a package of early learning and childcare measures in the 2024/25 financial year. £7.1m of the £25m is to enable stabilisation and some expansion of existing early years programmes, including Sure Start.  It is planned to commence implementation on receipt of the associated funding from the Department of Finance.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline their plans to implement the Period Products (Free Provision) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: From Monday 13 May 2024 TEO, in partnership with Libraries NI, we are providing free period products throughout the Libraries NI network of 96 static and 11 mobile libraries. 				Officials will monitor and evaluate the provision of products going forward and will engage with potential additional delivery partners.				Regulations will be made in due course that will specify the Department’s Public Bodies which will be under a duty to make free products available for use on their premises.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people awaiting a domiciliary care package to enable hospital discharge in the Western Health and Social Trust.
answer_text: Information on the number of patients in hospital unable to be discharged as they were awaiting a domiciliary care package to be put in place is not held centrally. This has been provided by the Western Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust for the position at 01 May 2024 and is detailed in table 1 overleaf.Table 1: Number of in-patients awaiting a domiciliary care package to enable hospital discharge in the Western Health and Social Care Trust, broken down by hospital, for the position on 1st May 2024 HospitalNumber of inpatientsAltnagelvin Area Hospital4South West Acute Hospital13Waterside Hospital4Omagh Hospital & Primary Care Centre2Total23Source: Western Health and Social Care TrustDomiciliary care services continue to experience significant pressures across the region, and there are a range of factors currently impacting upon delivery of this service; this includes significant retention issues in regard to domiciliary care staff.Where delivery of care packages has been negatively impacted due to challenges regarding capacity, Trusts have in place mitigation measures to try to ensure services continue to be provided. Since September 2022, measures have been in place for hospital discharge where a domiciliary care package cannot be provided on a timely basis. In such circumstances, individuals should be offered a place (without charge) in appropriate residential or nursing accommodation on a temporary basis if their needs cannot be met safely at home without some form of support.	In recognition of the challenges facing the independent social care sector, I recently announced a £70m funding package across a range of service provision, including domiciliary care. The purpose of this funding is to ensure independent sector providers have the necessary funding to meet the challenges of increases to national living wage from the beginning of April.  I have also established structures to consider how we move the sector to a Real Living Wage employer base and significant work is ongoing to build the case for the additional funding that will be required to deliver this.	There is clearly a longer term fundamental need to deliver reform and transformation in Health and Social Care. In light of this my Department has established the Adult Social Care Collaborative Forum. There are a number of workstreams in progress, including one specifically considering maximising capacity within domiciliary care services. Work is ongoing to develop a plan to grow capacity in domiciliary care across the region; a new model for domiciliary care, and to take forward key aspects of social care workforce development.My Department remains committed to the provision of a high quality domiciliary care service, and will continue to work alongside Trusts and independent sector providers to maintain and support service provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the 20mph speed limit zones outside schools; and (ii) whether he will give consideration to delivering the scheme to St Dympna's Primary School, Dromore.
answer_text: There have been two tranches of part time 20mph speed limits delivered, which introduced these measures at 215 schools, an investment of about £4m.  A review is underway to determine if a further tranche of the 20mph schools programme is justified. The findings of this review will be made public later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of the 3000 women impacted in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust, (i) whether there are any women in the Western Health and Social Care Trust who have experienced delays and reviews of their cervical smear test in the last six months; and (ii) how he plans to assist these women going forward.
answer_text: Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT) has advised the Department that a backlog of cases, which had built up prior to December 2023, for reporting cervical screening results has been cleared.  The WHSCT are now operating with a turnaround reporting time of 2 weeks for those who have a negative HPV test, in line with national guidance for primary HPV testing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail the (a) public holidays; and (b) bank holidays that are approved for staff to work overtime to help address the backlog of MOT testing; and (ii) whether he intends to extend overtime opportunities.
answer_text: In each of the last two years the Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) have conducted over 1.1 million vehicle tests which are the highest numbers ever recorded.  This increase in capacity has been achieved through a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, with the latest recruitment competition currently being progressed to fill vacancies across our network of test centres.  The DVA also use overtime to provide cover for leave and offer vehicle test appointments on Sundays and public/bank holidays when testing is not normally available. In 2023, there were only seven days in the year when they did not offer MOT appointments.In 2024 to date, the DVA has offered MOT appointments on all days, except New Year’s Day and Easter Sunday.  The DVA will continue to offer overtime on Sundays and public/bank holidays until vehicle test waiting times have significantly reduced.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the business case for the Mother and Baby Unit at the Belfast City Hospital.
answer_text: A business case for the proposed Mother and Baby Unit at the Belfast City Hospital site is currently being prepared. The timescale for approval of this business case remains subject to the identification of capital and revenue funding to deliver the unit in the context of an overall challenging financial position.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the social housing waiting list in the Dromore constituent ward of the West Tyrone District Electoral area; and (ii) what plans he has to construct new-build social housing in the Dromore constituent ward.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as per Annex A which has been laid in the library due to the volume of information.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans his Department has in place to help GP practices when the local Health and Social Care Trust is unable to provide the necessary support.
answer_text: DoH/SPPG are currently working with GP Practice, GP Federation and Health & Social Care Trust colleagues in order to identify and take forward long-term solutions for GP Practices in need of support.  DoH/SPPG continue to work with all GP Practices across Northern Ireland to maintain the provision of GMS services to the resident population.  Initiatives such as the introduction of General Practice Pharmacists and Advanced Nurse Practitioners are available to many practices across Northern Ireland.  DoH/SPPG have also recently commissioned an Attract, Recruit, Retain scheme to support GP Practices to attract, recruit and retain GPs into their practice team.DoH/SPPG also see the Multi-Disciplinary Team model as a key to stabilising and strengthening Primary Care services to ensure they can continue to provide high quality care to our people, now and into the future.  At this time, given the current, challenging, budgetary position, DoH are unable to confirm when the proposed further rollout of the MDT Programme might begin in other areas.  However, DoH are keen to see the MDT programme expand across Northern Ireland as quickly as possible to ensure more of our population can benefit from this approach.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on plans for the expansion of SureStart services in rural areas, particularly in Dromore, Co. Tyrone.
answer_text: My Department's Sure Start programme is available to around 41,500 children and their families living in (at least) the 25% most disadvantaged areas in Northern Ireland.Expansion of early years interventions currently funded by my Department (including Sure Start) is being considered within the development of an Executive Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) strategy, following stabilisation of existing provision. As I have previously made clear, any extended provision will be subject to availability of the required funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on Development Proposal 717, regarding the transformation of St. Eugene's Primary School, Tircur, to controlled integrated status.
To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the plans to assist and manage community GP practices when they are threatened with closure; and (ii) how he plans to assist them going forward.
answer_text: DoH/SPPG recognise that being able to access GP practices when we need to do so is important for all of us.  However, GP practices are under significant pressures at present as they deal with immediate demands and the consequences of wider service pressures.  DoH/SPPG continue to work with all GP Practices across Northern Ireland to maintain the provision of GMS services to the resident population. Initiatives such as the introduction of General Practice Pharmacists and Advanced Nurse Practitioners are available to many practices across Northern Ireland.  DoH/SPPG have also commissioned an Attract, Recruit, Retain scheme to support GP Practices to attract, recruit and retain GPs into their practice team.The GP Federation led General Practice Improvement and Crisis Response Team scheme is also available, which aims to support GP Practices at risk, to develop and implement an effective Practice Recovery Plan to ensure delivery of contractual obligations to their registered patients. This will include advice on management issues and GP clinical cover.DoH/SPPG also see the Multi-Disciplinary Team model as a key to stabilising and strengthening Primary Care services to ensure they can continue to provide high quality care to our people, now and into the future.  At this time, given the current, challenging, budgetary position, DoH are unable to confirm when the proposed further rollout of the MDT Programme might begin in other areas.  However, DoH are keen to see the MDT programme expand across Northern Ireland as quickly as possible to ensure more of our population can benefit from this approach.With regards to plans going forward when GP Practices are threatened with closure, DoH/SPPG are currently working closely with GP colleagues, GP Federations and Health & Social Care Trust colleagues in order to identify and take forward long-term solutions for GP Practices who are threatened with closure and/or are in need of support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the timeframe for when she intends to introduce hate crime legislation in this mandate.
answer_text: I am currently considering legislative priorities for inclusion in a Legislative Programme to be agreed by the Executive and notified to the Assembly in due course. The loss of two years of Assembly business time and the constraint on resources, both within the Department and the wider justice system, mean that the range of legislative reforms planned will be more restricted than originally planned.The final content of the Hate Crime Bill, due for introduction in the latter half of this mandate, is yet to be decided; however, it is my intention that this foundational Bill will give effect to a number of recommendations from the review of hate crime legislation in Northern Ireland, led by Judge Marrinan. The Phase 2 public consultation on further policy issues will take place later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the progress of a finalised Environment Strategy to be put forward as an Environmental Improvement Plan.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether DAERA Direct offices will have the appropriate number of trained staff to provide a high-quality service to farmers who visit these offices; (ii) what actions he will put in place to ensure that all front-line staff have an appropriate level of training and understanding of new systems such as NIFAIS to assist farmers.
answer_text: Despite the challenging resource position, staffing levels, training needs and work priorities in Customer Services Branch are regularly monitored to ensure sufficient trained staff are in place to deliver customer services to the highest standard possible in the DAERA Direct Offices. 	A robust training structure has been embedded in DAERA Direct Offices, including the establishment of a dedicated training team, to ensure staff receive relevant training in their day-to-day duties and specific training on new systems including NIFAIS.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission what plans it has to provide a creche facility for staff members.
answer_text: At its meeting on 5 November 2019, the Assembly Commission was advised that the then Permanent Secretary of the Department of Finance, had liaised with the Assembly’s Clerk/Chief Executive, to explore the feasibility of opening a pre-school childcare facility on the Stormont Estate. The intention was that such a facility could be used by Members, Members’ staff, Assembly Commission staff and Civil Service staff working on the Estate.The Department had advised that, at the time, the feasibility of a childcare facility on the Stormont Estate could have been considered as a potential action for inclusion in a draft Childcare Strategy which was being developed by the Department of Education.  In November 2022, the Department informed the Assembly Commission that work on the Childcare Strategy had been paused due to the pandemic, and that it was unknown if the Strategy would now include the provision of a childcare facility, due to the significant changes of the Civil Service workforce within the Estate, notably with regards to Hybrid working.  Subsequently, the Department confirmed in September 2023 that it now had no immediate plans for a childcare facility on the Stormont Estate.  The Assembly Commission has not considered the matter further since.  The Assembly Commission remains open to exploring cooperation on such a project but without other partners, the viability of the proposal is questionable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the availability of the allergy treatment Palforzia across each Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) how his Department intends to ensure this drug is available to all who need it.
answer_text: Officers within the Department and its SPPG remain committed to the introduction of a local service to provide Palforzia to patients within Northern Ireland.  Work is ongoing to agree the most appropriate service delivery model for Northern Ireland.  In the interim, Palforzia is available on a case by case basis for eligible patients in line with the Department’s endorsed guidance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he intends to reinstate the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive.
answer_text: Reducing waiting times for elective access is a key priority for me. Current waiting times are unacceptable, no one should have to wait longer than necessary for the surgery they need.  While good progress has been made in tackling waiting times in some areas, there remains much more to be done. Within the constraints of existing budgets, I’m committed to taking whatever actions I can to tackle these waiting times and ensure that patients are treated as quickly as possible to ensure best possible outcomes.As you will be aware, I introduced the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme (RoIRS) on 30 June 2021 as part of the Elective Care Framework to help reduce NI waiting lists. The scheme was a limited 12-month administrative version of the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive (CBHD) which ceased to apply to NI following the UK’s exit from the EU. In June 2022, I announced that the RoIRS would be extended beyond the planned 30 June 2022 deadline, until an additional £5m of new funding had been committed. The RoIRS closed to new applications on Wednesday 21 September 2022 when the additional £5m funding had been exhausted. Given the pressing demands for funding across many areas of health and social care no further funding could be assigned to this scheme.Any future funding allocation to a similar scheme to help reduce waiting lists will now need to be considered as part of a broader review of resource investment into reducing waiting lists.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will set up a scheme to provide financial assistance to those who paid for their own orthopedic surgery due to lengthy waiting lists.
answer_text: At present there are no patient driven funding schemes offered by Health and Social Care (HSC) for private treatment received following the closure of the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme (RoIRS). Any future funding allocation to a similar scheme to help reduce waiting lists will now need to be considered as part of a broader review of resource investment into reducing waiting lists.When in operation, the RoIRS was subject to strict criteria which are set out and available on the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) website: (https://online.hscni.net/travelfortreatment/roi-reimbursement/). The guidance clearly states that the SPPG will reimburse treatment costs under the RoIRS if the patient had sought and received prior approval from the SPPG for the treatment under the scheme. It advises that retrospective applications will not be considered. The SPPG required that patients sought prior approval for two main reasons: to ensure that such patients met the same thresholds and qualifying criteria as those seeking treatment in the HSC system; and, to protect the patient from the financial consequences of purchasing treatment for which they may not have received reimbursement. In Northern Ireland, all patients on a hospital waiting list are managed chronologically based on their clinical need, with urgent patients seen and treated first. Management of waiting lists is one of the mechanisms that upholds the principle of equity of access to HSC services. However, where a patient chooses to pay for private treatment, they do so in the understanding that this takes them outside the HSC system’s arrangements and its principles. The current policy is that patients cannot be reimbursed for expenses incurred in private treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of how the A5 upgrade will impact on road safety.
To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the status of the linked Development Proposal 706 and 707 for Tummery Primary School and St. Dympna's Primary School, Co. Tyrone.
answer_text: Development Proposals (DPs) 706 and 707 propose that Tummery Primary School (PS) will discontinue with effect from 31 August 2025 and that St Dympna’s PS, Dromore will increase its admission and enrolment numbers from 29 to 39 and from 205 to 273 respectively, with effect from 1 September 2025.These joint proposals were published by the Education Authority on behalf of the Council for Catholic Maintained Schools (CCMS) on 13 April 2023 and the statutory objection periods ended on 20 June 2023.The proposals are part of a strategic area solution by the CCMS for the Dromore area.Officials have gathered and analysed all pertinent information in relation to the proposals and the evidence will be presented to me in due course for a decision.I am aware of the need to make a timely decision when these have been presented to me as I appreciate the importance of bringing clarity and certainty for all those associated with the schools concerned.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how will he address the poor registration rates for dentists in Fermanagh and Omagh District Council area.
answer_text: A key part of the access issues facing patients are the rates being paid to our dentists for treatment.  The absence of Executive Ministers has meant that it has not been possible to progress the recommended pay uplifts for staffing groups for 2023/24.  This is now my immediate priority, and I am meeting with Trade Unions in early course to take this forward. Officials have already been making arrangements in anticipation of funding being made available to ensure that uplifts can be implemented as quickly as possible.I know that an additional £95m has been invested in General Dental Services via the Financial Support Scheme and Rebuilding Support Scheme since April 2020. This came to an end in July 2023 due to the severe financial pressures arising from the Department’s 2023/24 budget settlement.A pilot scheme is currently running to provide appointments to unregistered patients who require emergency dental treatment during the working week. If a patient is not registered with a dentist, requires emergency dental treatment, and has been unable to access an appointment, they can contact their local Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) office for advice.I am also conscious of the progress that has been made in the rest of the UK on dental reform and I will be discussing these issues with officials as soon as possible, to ensure that the sector here in NI is supported in delivering important care to patients. More immediately, officials have been developing investment proposals to further support General Dental Services and I will consider them in early course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many calls Omagh based Ambulances have responded to, at locations in the Omagh district, in the last eight months.
To ask the Minister of Education whether there has been any progress in the commissioning of research by her Department to inform the development of a childcare strategy.
answer_text: The contract for an independent research report on a Review of Childcare Services in NI was awarded on 29 June and will run to December 2022.  On completion of the final report, arrangements for its publication will be made.	I am aware of the cost pressures currently being faced by childcare providers, along with many other sectors, and appreciate the importance of childcare services remaining available to those who need them.  Sustainability is therefore being considered as part of the ongoing development work on the Executive Childcare Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following the announcement that the Western Health and Social Care Trust is taking on the contract temporarily for the Dromore and Trillick GP Practice in Tyrone, what measures he is taking to ensure GP services are protected on a long-term basis.
answer_text: Primary care services are a cornerstone of our health and social care provision.  Unfortunately, even prior to the COVID-19 pandemic, primary care services were already coming under increasing pressure.  It remains my assessment that GP-led services cannot continue to meet the rising demand and expectations without urgent action to address the pressures facing the service.  To do this, means that we have to focus on both the GP workforce and on developing new ways of working that were already being implemented but which have accelerated during the pandemic. Work is underway on a review of GP trainee places with a working group established to explore issues relating to the GP workforce to ensure we have the workforce in Primary Care we need going forward.  Whilst this work is ongoing, I have accepted the working group’s recommendation of an immediate increase of 10 additional GP training places as an interim measure for the 2022/23 academic year, bringing the total number in Northern Ireland to an all-time high of 121 training places this year. This builds on my Department’s continuing work to invest in our GP workforce which has seen the number of GPs trainees increase by over 70% from 2015 levels.  This will help stabilise our GP workforce and ensure it is sustainable for the future.  Any future increases will be informed by completion of the work of the working group and subject to the availability of funding.I recognise that GPs’ experiences during clinical placements can have a significant influence on their future career decisions.  In that regard, the commencement of the Graduate Entry Programme at the Ulster University’s Medical School is a significant development in the provision of medical education in Northern Ireland.  The curriculum of this Graduate Entry Programme places a very significant emphasis on Primary Care placements, with a high concentration of clinical placements in rural settings in the west.  This will help to increase the supply of local students who may be attracted to pursue a career in General Practice in these areas.In addition to the work outlined above, a number of support schemes are also in place.  The GP Induction and Refresher Scheme provides an opportunity for GPs who meet the required criteria to safely return to general practice following a career break or time spent working abroad. It also supports the safe introduction of overseas GPs and provides support to GPs returning to or entering clinical practice in Northern Ireland.  Similarly, the GP Retention Scheme is designed to assist in the retention of GPs who may be considering leaving or reducing their sessional commitment to general practice. It is also important that incoming GPs are reassured that they will be part of a wider primary care team since it has become increasingly evident in recent years that the increase in demand for primary care services cannot be met solely by increasing the number of GPs.  Other elements which my Department continues

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when the review of childcare provision undertaken by her Department will be published.
answer_text: The contract for an independent research report on a Review of Childcare Services in NI was awarded on 29 June and will run to December 2022.  On completion of the final report, arrangements for its publication will be made.	I am aware of the cost pressures currently being faced by childcare providers, along with many other sectors, and appreciate the importance of childcare services remaining available to those who need them.  Sustainability is therefore being considered as part of the ongoing development work on the Executive Childcare Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether her Department has allocated additional sustainability funding to childcare providers to help meet their higher energy bills during the current cost of living crisis.
answer_text: The contract for an independent research report on a Review of Childcare Services in NI was awarded on 29 June and will run to December 2022.  On completion of the final report, arrangements for its publication will be made.	I am aware of the cost pressures currently being faced by childcare providers, along with many other sectors, and appreciate the importance of childcare services remaining available to those who need them.  Sustainability is therefore being considered as part of the ongoing development work on the Executive Childcare Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether her Department has considered ways in which to incorporate emotional health and well-being and mental health awareness into the school curriculum.
answer_text: The emotional health and wellbeing of all our pupils remains a high priority for me.  This has been even more important through the pandemic which has significantly impacted on the lives of our children and young people.  Education continues to play a vital role in supporting children and young people’s wellbeing and for some of our most vulnerable acting as a stabilising refuge from other aspects of their lives outside school.Within the curriculum, mental health, self-esteem and wellbeing education is already included within the Personal Development and Mutual Understanding and Learning for Life and Work areas of learning.  Pupils are provided with opportunities to develop an understanding of how to maximise and sustain their mental health, self-esteem and wellbeing, including managing emotions and reactions to ongoing life experiences and enhancing self-awareness and developing coping strategies.   There are also a number of qualifications at Key Stage 4 and post-16 which provide opportunities for students to explore issues relating to self-esteem, mental health and wellbeing.The Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment provides curricular guidance and teaching support materials for use in schools on its Wellbeing Hub which contains a range of age-appropriate, up-to-date, relevant resources to support the emotional health and wellbeing of pupils.  Additionally, the joint Department of Education and Department of Health ‘Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework’ was published in February 2021.  This aims to:ensure children and young people are empowered and assisted to understand and take care / manage their emotional health and wellbeing;identify needs early and address effectively when required;establish an integrated model that supports an early help, support and intervention focus; andreduce the numbers of children and young people requiring specialist intervention from Mental Health Services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on (i) his Department's review of the Postgraduate Tuition Fee Loan; (ii) when he expects this review to be completed; (iii) whether he will give a commitment that any increase in funding will be provided ahead of the 2022/23 academic year; and (iv) when he expects the proposals from the review to be implemented.
answer_text: The public consultation on the Northern Ireland Postgraduate Tuition Fee Loan was published in April 2021 and ran until June 2021. This consultation explored both the level of funding available for tuition fee support and also the potential for different maintenance support systems.My Department is currently considering the responses to the consultation along with policy options, and I hope to publish a formal consultation response in the coming weeks. At that point I will be able to advise of any possible changes to postgraduate policy and funding, and indicate when these changes will be implemented. However, any outcomes, particularly in relation to the levels of financial support available, will be dependent on the funding that is available to the Department over the coming years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of people who have been supported with initial upfront childcare costs through the Advisor Discretion Fund, since it was extended in October 2021; (ii) the number of people who have submitted a claim and been rejected; (iii) the average amount of support that has been awarded; and (iv) the average length of time it has taken to process an application for support.
answer_text: Since 25 October 2021, I have made Upfront Childcare Costs available under the Adviser Discretion Fund to help parents to start work, sustain and/or increase their hours or return to work following maternity leave. Since then, and up until the 20 June 2022, my Department has:supported 330 people;rejected no applications as eligibility is established by discussion between the customer and their Work Coach before an application is made;awarded an average of £569; andtaken an average of 16 calendar days to process an application for support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he intends to extend the Cross Border Health Directive beyond 1 July 2022.
answer_text: You will be aware that in June 2021, I announced a new Cross-Border Healthcare Scheme (ROI Reimbursement Scheme) for a 12 month period from 30th June 2021 as the Cross Border Healthcare Directive no longer applies to the UK.  This initiative, part of the Elective Care Framework, is aimed at reducing Northern Ireland waiting lists.  The scheme has been very successful, with significant patient interest. From July 1st 2021 to the end of April 2022 there were 2,842 applications received and 2,246 applications already approved.  By comparison in the 2020-21 year, prior to the introduction of the new scheme there were 1,466 applications to use the previous Cross Border Healthcare Directive throughout the EU, of which 886 were for the provision of services in RoI. Some 89% of the applications to date to the ROIRS relate to orthopaedics, the vast majority of which relate to hip and knee replacements. Therefore the scheme has provided valuable orthopaedic capacity as the service locally rebuilds. As HSC capacity is continuing to grow I have therefore taken the decision to extend the ROIRS by allocating a further £5m to permit its continued utilisation beyond the planned June 30 2022 deadline.  New applications can be accepted from 1st July 2022.  These will be processed in chronological order and scheme closure will be deferred until the £5m funding has been committed. This is funding that I do not currently have in my Department’s baseline so it is a decision that I have taken at risk, however given the obvious benefits of the scheme it was one I felt was necessary as essential HSC capacity continues to become increasingly available over the coming weeks and months.
